The Porter and the Sailor.definition: Being friendly and generous to guests — making someone feel welcome in your home.
A large amount of money or wealth — also used to mean the luck or success a person has in life.

The First Voyage: The Island That Moved.definition: Goods or products carried by a ship from one place to another to be sold.
A journey on a ship — also means paying for a spot on a ship to travel somewhere.

The Second Voyage: The Valley of Diamond.definition: Going down from a high place to a lower one — moving downward.
A person who buys and sells goods to make money — a trader.

The Third Voyage: The Giant's Island.definition: Clever at solving problems, especially in tricky or dangerous situations.
A safe place to go when you are in danger — shelter from something harmful.

The Fourth Voyage: The Underground Kingd.definition: A tradition or habit that people in a certain place do regularly — the way things are normally done there.
To stay alive through something dangerous or difficult.

The Fifth Voyage: The Old Man of the Sea.definition: To trick someone into believing something that is not true.
Extremely tired — having used up all of your energy.

The Sixth Voyage: The River Under the Mo.definition: The steady movement of water in one direction — like a river flowing from one place to another.
To save or collect useful things from a wreck or disaster.

A Night Off: Hindbad Asks Questions.definition: To put up with something hard or painful without giving up.
To arrange people or things in a way that helps get something done.

The Seventh Voyage: The King's Letter.definition: A person sent to represent their country or ruler in another land.
A person who has been caught and is being held by someone else — a prisoner.

Back to the Beginning.definition: A ticket or spot on a ship for traveling — also the journey itself.
A feeling of being thankful for something kind that someone did for you.

The Ship That Did Not Wait.definition: The uncomfortable feeling you get when you have done something foolish or awkward.
Something that absolutely must happen — a situation where you have no choice but to act.

What the Giant Taught Me.definition: The desire to hurt someone on purpose — wanting to cause harm.
A feeling that something might be wrong or not what it seems.

The City Under the Mountain Sky.definition: Extremely large — bigger than you can easily imagine.
A person who studies the stars, planets, and sky.

The Letter the Caliph Sent.definition: Having stopped working, usually because you are old enough or have earned enough money.
Money paid to free a person who has been captured.

The Elephant and the Secret Valley.definition: The hard white material that makes up an elephant's tusks — very valuable and used for carving.
To talk with someone to reach an agreement that works for both sides.

The Long Way Home.definition: Deep sadness, especially from losing someone you care about.
The feeling that something is fully finished — brought to its proper end.

What Wealth Actually Costs.definition: Something that makes it easier for one person than another — a helpful head start.
The money you earn after paying back what something cost you.

The King of Serendib Remembers.definition: Having the ability to do something — strong or skilled enough.
The feeling of being safe and protected from danger or hardship.

The Shape of the Sea.definition: Not caring one way or the other — having no feelings about something.
To find your way from one place to another, especially on water or in the wilderness.

What Was Lost.definition: Being in charge of something and having to answer for what happens to it.
A deep sadness that stays with you after losing something or someone important.

The Merchants He Met.definition: An inn along a trade road where travelers could rest, eat, and shelter for the night.
Something or someone you can count on to do what they say — dependable.

The Last Thing I Learned.definition: Happy and satisfied with what you have — not needing anything more.
Worth noticing because it is unusual or impressive.

Morning at the Sailor's House.definition: Having the skill or strength to do something difficult.
Someone who sees or hears something important and can tell others about it.

The Purse of Gold.definition: Willing to give more than expected — sharing freely with others.
The feeling of seeing something true in another person — understanding each other.
